subject: Internal Medicine
text: Occlusion of the left anterior descending artery would most likely produce ECG changes in?
explanation: LAD occlusion causes anterior wall MI, reflected in leads V1-V4.
educationalObjective: Localize coronary artery occlusion by ECG leads.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Which of the following is a contraindication to performing an exercise tolerance test?
explanation: Exercise testing is contraindicated within 2-5 days after acute MI due to risk of arrhythmias or reinfarction.
educationalObjective: Recognize contraindications to exercise stress testing.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 57-year-old man presents with chest pain and shortness of breath. ECG confirms anterior MI. Post-MI course complicated by heart failure. In which scenario would nitroglycerin be most appropriate initial medication?
options: moderate pulmonary congestion, BP 130 mm Hg
severe pulmonary congestion, BP 80 mm Hg
correctAnswer: moderate pulmonary congestion, BP 130 mm Hg
explanation: Nitroglycerin reduces preload and afterload; beneficial in pulmonary congestion with adequate BP (≥90-100 mmHg). Avoid in hypotension.
educationalObjective: Appropriate use of nitroglycerin in acute heart failure post-MI.
hint: Needs adequate BP and signs of congestion.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Which heart murmur typically diminishes in intensity during pregnancy?
explanation: Decreased systemic vascular resistance in pregnancy reduces the intensity of aortic regurgitation murmur.
educationalObjective: Physiological changes in heart murmurs during pregnancy.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: During evaluation of JVP, which condition is associated with a prominent y descent?
explanation: Prominent y descent (Friedreich's sign) occurs in constrictive pericarditis due to rapid ventricular filling in early diastole.
educationalObjective: Interpret JVP waveforms in cardiac diseases.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: In diastolic dysfunction with preserved LVEF, which condition could be present?
explanation: Chronic hypertension leads to LV hypertrophy and diastolic dysfunction with preserved EF (HFpEF).
educationalObjective: Identify causes of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 54-year-old male presents with chest pain radiating to epigastrium, ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, aVF. What is the immediate management?
explanation: Inferior STEMI: primary PCI is preferred if available within 90-120 min; otherwise fibrinolytic therapy.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 55-year-old male with chest pain radiating to left arm, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea <2 hours. Initial reperfusion management?
explanation: Primary PCI within 90 minutes of first medical contact is preferred reperfusion for STEMI.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Mr. Salam has recurrent bloody diarrhea over several weeks. Suspect ulcerative colitis. What advice would you give?
options: He may require sigmoidoscopy followed by colonoscopy
Definitive treatment would be given straightforward irrespective of colonic biopsy
Total colectomy may be required at a later stage
explanation: Ulcerative colitis typically has a relapsing-remitting course. All other options may be true except that treatment depends on biopsy and severity.
educationalObjective: Understand the natural history and management of UC.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Mr. Karim has chronic diarrhea. Which advice is the exception (not correct)?
options: He shall take anti-motility drugs regularly for symptomatic relief
Chronic diarrhea could be due to giardiasis
A routine stool exam is a must
He may need thyroid function tests
correctAnswer: He shall take anti-motility drugs regularly for symptomatic relief
explanation: Anti-motility drugs are not first-line for chronic undiagnosed diarrhea; they can mask serious conditions. Workup should precede symptomatic treatment.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 46-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents with bilateral leg swelling, eGFR >60, urinalysis protein 4+, blood negative, protein:creatinine ratio 1680 mg/mmol, serum albumin 14 g/L. Previous values 4 months ago normal. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Rapid onset of nephrotic syndrome with normal renal function and no hematuria suggests minimal change disease, which can occur in adults, including diabetics.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Signs of fluid overload in glomerulonephritis include all except?
options: Edema and hypertension due to fluid overload (in 75% of patients)
explanation: Proteinuria is a manifestation of glomerular injury, not fluid overload. The others are signs of volume overload.
educationalObjective: Identify signs of fluid overload vs glomerular injury.
hint: Proteinuria is the disease, not the overload.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A patient with ulcerative colitis presents with abdominal pain, jaundice, fatigue, elevated ALP. MRCP shows fibrosis and strictures in intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: PSC is strongly associated with ulcerative colitis and causes multifocal bile duct strictures seen on MRCP.
educationalObjective: Recognize PSC as a complication of IBD.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A patient with dyspepsia, no family history of cancer. Most appropriate advice?
options: Reassure the patient regarding signs and symptoms
Refer the patient to a gastroenterologist
Reassure about lack of family history of cancer
Send for check peptic ulcer disease
correctAnswer: Reassure the patient regarding signs and symptoms
explanation: In young patients without alarm features, reassurance and lifestyle advice are appropriate; no need for immediate referral or invasive testing.
educationalObjective: Management of dyspepsia without red flags.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: 65-year-old male with hematemesis, pale, short of breath. Best initial step?
options: Check vital signs and amount of blood loss
correctAnswer: Check vital signs and amount of blood loss
explanation: Initial management of acute upper GI bleeding: ABCs – assess hemodynamic stability, then resuscitation. Endoscopy is after stabilization.
educationalObjective: Initial approach to acute GI bleeding.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Patient with sustained epigastric pain for 3 weeks, OTC no relief. Most appropriate advice?
explanation: Persistent symptoms despite OTC treatment warrant specialist referral for endoscopy and further workup.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A urinalysis reveals muddy brown granular casts. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Muddy brown granular casts are characteristic of ATN.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A patient with Stage 3 CKD, hypertension, proteinuria. Most appropriate antihypertensive?
explanation: ACEi/ARBs are first-line in CKD with proteinuria due to renoprotective effects.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: 48-year-old male with sepsis, creatinine 2.5 mg/dl. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Sepsis is a common cause of acute kidney injury (pre-renal or intrinsic).
educationalObjective: Differentiate AKI from CKD in acute setting.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Which diagnostic method differentiates Prerenal from Renal AKI?
explanation: FeNa <1% suggests prerenal; >2% suggests intrinsic ATN.
educationalObjective: Use FeNa to distinguish prerenal from intrinsic AKI.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Drug-induced nephrotoxicity occurs more frequently in patients with?
explanation: Volume depletion reduces renal perfusion, concentrating drugs and increasing risk of nephrotoxicity.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Pathophysiology of renal hypertension involves?
explanation: Renal artery stenosis or parenchymal disease leads to renin-angiotensin-aldosterone activation, causing hypertension.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 55-year-old female with acute hepatitis A. Most useful and rapid indicator of altered hepatic synthetic function?
explanation: PT/INR reflects synthesis of clotting factors (half-life hours) and changes rapidly, whereas albumin has longer half-life.
educationalObjective: Evaluate synthetic function in acute liver injury.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 28-year-old man with mild intermittent jaundice during fasting or stress. Total bilirubin 2.5 mg/dL (unconjugated). Normal LFTs. Father had similar. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Gilbert's syndrome is benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia worsened by fasting/stress, with normal LFTs and no hemolysis.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: To provide immediate passive protection against Hepatitis B to a newborn of HBsAg-positive mother, give?
options: Active immunization with recombinant hepatitis B vaccine only
explanation: HBIG provides passive immunity immediately, while vaccine takes time to induce active immunity. Both are given together.
educationalObjective: Prophylaxis for perinatal hepatitis B exposure.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A pregnant woman HBsAg positive, HBc IgG positive, HBeAg mild positive, HBsAg positive for 15 years. Correct diagnosis?
explanation: HBsAg >6 months indicates chronic hepatitis B. HBeAg positivity suggests active viral replication.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Patient with acute liver failure of unknown etiology. Which medication to administer in all cases?
explanation: NAC is beneficial in all-cause acute liver failure (not just acetaminophen) due to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Cirrhotic patient with ascites, abdominal pain, fever, PMN count 300 cells/ml. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Ascitic fluid PMN ≥250 cells/ml in cirrhotic patient indicates SBP.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 56-year-old man with community-acquired pneumonia, despite 10 days of antibiotics, remains febrile with fluctuant chest wall swelling. Chest X-ray shows loculated pleural collection extending to soft tissues. Likely complication?
explanation: Empyema necessitans is when pleural infection extends through chest wall to subcutaneous tissues.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: What is the best initial test to confirm asthma diagnosis?
explanation: Reversible airflow obstruction (increase in FEV1 ≥12% and ≥200 mL) confirms asthma.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Which is a poor prognostic factor in COPD?
explanation: Frequent exacerbations accelerate lung function decline, increase mortality, and are a poor prognostic indicator.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 25-year-old male with acute asthma attack: severe dyspnea, wheezing, O2 sat 88%, unable to speak in full sentences. Most appropriate initial management?
explanation: Severe asthma exacerbation requires oxygen, repeated SABA, and systemic corticosteroids. IV steroids are indicated in severe cases.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Which occupational lung disease is most strongly associated with increased risk of lung cancer?
explanation: Asbestos exposure is a strong risk factor for lung cancer and mesothelioma.
educationalObjective: Occupational lung diseases and cancer risk.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 55-year-old male chest X-ray reveals straight left heart border and double cardiac shadow to right of sternum. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Straight left heart border on PA view is due to enlarged left atrial appendage; double density sign is also due to left atrial enlargement.
educationalObjective: Radiographic signs of left atrial enlargement.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 24-year-old male with tall stature, eunuchoid habitus, sparse pubic hair, small firm testes (1.5 cm). FSH & LH elevated, testosterone low. Next step?
explanation: Elevated gonadotropins with hypogonadism suggests primary testicular failure; karyotyping for Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY) is diagnostic.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Pathophysiology of gynecomastia?
options: Increased levels of endogenous free androgens
Increased androgen insensitivity of breast tissue
Decreased sensitivity of breast tissue to estrogens
explanation: Gynecomastia results from increased estrogen action or decreased androgen action relative to estrogen in breast tissue.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Patient with short fourth metacarpals and metatarsals. Correct statement?
explanation: Short fourth metacarpals and metatarsals (Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy) are seen in pseudohypoparathyroidism, which is associated with hypocalcemia and elevated PTH due to end-organ resistance.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A patient presents with central obesity, abdominal striae, proximal myopathy. Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test: morning cortisol 310 nmol/L (normal <50). Next step to confirm diagnosis?
explanation: To differentiate pituitary from adrenal/ectopic Cushing's, high-dose DST is used. Failure to suppress suggests adrenal or ectopic ACTH.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 32-year-old man with polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss. Which statement about type 1 DM is wrong?
options: HLA haplotypes DR3/DR4 on chromosome 6 increase susceptibility
At diagnosis, 80-90% of β cells destroyed
In insulitis, alpha cells are destroyed while others remain intact
explanation: DR3 and DR4 increase susceptibility, not decrease. Alpha cells are not destroyed in insulitis; beta cells are targeted.
educationalObjective: Understand genetic and pathological features of type 1 diabetes.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Patient 3 weeks after pharyngitis develops fever, new pansystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla, elevated ESR. Echocardiography shows valvular regurgitation without vegetations. Most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Post-streptococcal acute rheumatic fever can cause mitral regurgitation (Jones criteria). Absence of vegetations makes endocarditis less likely.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: Patient presents with hoarseness and mild stridor, left vocal cord paralysis. Most common cause?
options: Compression of the aorta on left recurrent laryngeal nerve
Upper airway obstruction at right recurrent laryngeal nerve
correctAnswer: Compression of the aorta on left recurrent laryngeal nerve
explanation: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve passes under aortic arch; aortic aneurysm or cardiomegaly can cause compression, leading to vocal cord paralysis.
educationalObjective: Identify causes of unilateral vocal cord paralysis.

subject: Internal Medicine
text: A 25-year-old man with fever, malaise, dyspnea, new cardiac murmur, splenomegaly. Blood cultures positive for Streptococcus gallolyticus. Next step?
explanation: S. gallolyticus (bovis) bacteremia/endocarditis is strongly associated with colonic neoplasms; colonoscopy is indicated.
educationalObjective: Association between S. gallolyticus and colorectal cancer.
